---
title: "Computing for the Social Sciences: Lecture 5"
subtitle: "Topics: Week 1 Review"
author: "Sabrina Nardin, Summer 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    incremental: false
    css: ../style/styles.css
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
    ratio: 16:9
    chalkboard: true
    toc: false
    center: true
---

# Agenda {.center}

::: {.agenda-list}
1. Quiz 1 & HW 1 Q&A
2. Breakout activity: Week 1 review teams
3. Individual Q&A
:::


## Today's plan

Today is a review class!

We will use Week 1 materials, your quiz responses, and group discussion to make the Week 1 tools feel more connected and manageable before we move on.

Warm-up Poll Everywhere: "What is one thing from Week 1 that still feels fuzzy?" 

<!--
Examples:

- Git vs GitHub
- Commit / push / pull
- Workbench
- R Markdown chunks
- Knitting/rendering
- `ggplot()`
- `aes()`
- Choosing graph types
-->


# 1. Quiz 1 and HW1 Review

Let's go over Quiz 1 responses (from Canvas) and HW1 questions
<!--
I looked at the quiz responses, and today we will go over it. 
I’m not reviewing the quiz to catch mistakes. I’m using it as a diagnostic tool: 
if many people missed something, that means it is worth reviewing together. 
-->


# 2. Breakout Activity: Week 1 Review


---

## Breakout rooms

You will work in **breakout groups of 3-4 participants**. Each breakout room will focus on **one Week 1 lecture**. We will repeat lectures across rooms, so more than one group may review the same lecture.

| Breakout room | Focus |
|---|---|
| Room 1 | Lecture 1: Course Logistics; Intro to R & Markdown |
| Room 2 | Lecture 2: Git & GitHub with RStudio |
| Room 3 | Lecture 3: Intro to `ggplot2`; Coding Style |
| Room 4 | Lecture 4: Graphs for Data Analysis with `ggplot2` |
| Room 5 | Lecture 1: Course Logistics; Intro to R & Markdown |
| Room 6 | Lecture 2: Git & GitHub with RStudio |
| Room 7 | Lecture 3: Intro to `ggplot2`; Coding Style |
| Room 8 | Lecture 4: Graphs for Data Analysis with `ggplot2` |

If we have additional rooms, we will keep repeating this pattern.

---

## What to do in your group

Open [this Google Doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1cULyt1-FjGhsKF-dVg_Dy1bAJ1M6j_0LOhQudqfi2Do/edit?usp=sharing) and follow the instructions there.

You will have about **12-15 minutes** in breakout rooms.

By the end, your group should have a completed row in the Google Doc. When we return, each group will give a short teach-back.



# 3. Individual Q&A

Stay in this Zoom room after class if you have more questions or would like individual help!


<!--

### Lecture 1: Course Logistics; Intro to R & Markdown

Possible topics:

- How this course is organized
- R as a programming language
- Markdown vs R Markdown
- Code chunks vs regular text
- Knitting/rendering
- File names and file organization
- Asking questions and using course materials

Possible common confusion:

- Writing code in regular text instead of inside a code chunk
- Thinking that knitting/rendering is the same as saving

### Lecture 2: Git & GitHub with RStudio

Possible topics:

- Git vs GitHub
- Local repository vs remote repository
- Commit, push, and pull
- Workbench/RStudio projects
- Homework assignment workflow
- Why we use version control

Possible common confusion:

- Git is not the same thing as GitHub
- Commit saves a version locally; push sends it to GitHub

### Lecture 3: Intro to `ggplot2`; Coding Style

Possible topics:

- `ggplot()` and layers
- `geom_point()`
- Mapping variables with `aes()`
- Setting vs mapping aesthetics
- The `+` sign in ggplot code
- Coding style and readable code

Possible common confusion:

- `aes(color = class)` maps color to a variable
- `color = "blue"` sets all points to blue
- `aes(color = "blue")` is usually not what we want

### Lecture 4: Graphs for Data Analysis with `ggplot2`

Possible topics:

- Exploratory data analysis
- Choosing graphs based on variable types
- Bar charts, histograms, boxplots, scatterplots
- Looking for patterns, outliers, and differences
- Keeping first plots simple
- Interpreting graphs carefully

Possible common confusion:

- Bar charts are usually for categorical variables
- Histograms are usually for continuous variables
- Scatterplots show relationships between two continuous variables

-->
